runs.interpretation: Primary-earner substitution-response elasticity for earnings decile 6 in a U.S. microsimulation model
citations: Chetty, R. (2012). Bounds on Elasticities with Optimization Frictions: A Synthesis of Micro and Macro Evidence on Labor Supply. Econometrica.
CBO (2012). Labor Supply and Fiscal Policy. Congressional Budget Office Working Paper.
Keane, M. (2011). Labor Supply and Taxes: A Survey. Journal of Economic Literature.
runs.reasoningSummary: Primary earners (typically male household heads) exhibit relatively inelastic labor supply compared to secondary earners. The literature consensus places compensated (substitution) elasticities for primary earners in the range of roughly 0.1–0.5, with central estimates around 0.2–0.3. Decile 6 represents middle-income earners who face moderate effective marginal tax rates under current U.S. law; there is no strong reason to expect a notably different elasticity from the average primary-earner value at this decile. Microsimulation models (e.g., those used by CBO or Tax Policy Center) typically parameterize primary-earner compensated elasticities around 0.25. Uncertainty spans the range of estimates across different studies and methodologies, justifying a moderate spread around the central value.

Decile 6 represents middle-income earners who face moderate effective marginal tax rates under current U.S. law; there is no strong reason to expect a notably different elasticity from the average primary-earner value at this decile. Microsimulation models (e.g., those used by CBO or Tax Policy Center) typically parameterize primary-earner compensated elasticities around 0.25. Uncertainty spans the range of estimates across different studies and methodologies, justifying a moderate spread around the central value."}

Decile 6 represents solidly middle-income workers who face moderate effective marginal tax rates and tend to have less labor-supply flexibility than secondary earners. Uncertainty remains about the precise decile-specific value, justifying a moderate spread around this central estimate.

citations: Chetty (2012, JEP) - survey of labor supply elasticities suggesting compensated elasticities for primary earners around 0.1–0.3
Keane (2011, JEL) - review finding primary-earner (male) substitution elasticities roughly 0.1–0.5 depending on specification
CBO microsimulation documentation (various years) - tends to apply primary-earner substitution elasticities in the 0.1–0.3 range for middle-income deciles
runs.reasoningSummary: Primary earners in decile 6 are solidly middle-income. The labor economics literature and standard U.S. microsimulation models (e.g., CBO-style) typically assign primary earners compensated (substitution) elasticities in the range of 0.1–0.4. Decile 6 workers are past the steepest part of the EITC phase-out and face moderate effective marginal rates; they are not at a kink with strong income effects pulling the other way. Consensus estimates cluster around 0.1–0.3 for primary earners, with some models using slightly higher values. I center my belief at 0.25 with moderate uncertainty, reflecting that the exact value is model- and study-specific.

runs.reasoningSummary: Primary earners (typically male household heads) have well-documented low-to-moderate labor supply elasticities. The broad consensus from the empirical literature places the compensated (substitution) elasticity for primary earners in the range of roughly 0.1–0.4, with central estimates near 0.2–0.3. Decile 6 sits in the middle of the earnings distribution—above the low-income range where phaseouts create high implicit marginal rates, and below the top where tax avoidance channels matter more. In a U.S. tax-benefit microsimulation context (e.g., CBO-style or TAXSIM-linked models), calibrated substitution elasticities for primary earners in middle deciles are typically set around 0.25, consistent with the Chetty (2012) meta-analytic bounds and standard labor-supply literature. Uncertainty spans roughly 0.05 to 0.50 reflecting heterogeneity across studies, identification strategies, and model specifications.

runs.reasoningSummary: Primary earners (predominantly full-time workers, often male) in the middle of the earnings distribution (decile 6) exhibit relatively inelastic labor supply on the intensive margin. The empirical consensus from meta-analyses (Chetty 2012, Keane 2011) places compensated/substitution elasticities for primary earners at roughly 0.1–0.4, with central estimates around 0.2–0.3. Decile 6 represents lower-middle to middle income, where budget constraints and hours norms are moderate. Tax-benefit microsimulations (e.g., CBO-style models) typically parameterize primary-earner substitution elasticities near 0.25 for this range. I center my belief at 0.25 with meaningful uncertainty spanning roughly 0.05 to 0.50 at the tails.

I center my belief at 0.25 with moderate uncertainty reflecting variation across modeling choices and empirical estimates.

runs.reasoningSummary: Primary earners in decile 6 (roughly middle-income, ~$60k–$80k range) are typically modeled with moderate compensated (substitution) labor-supply elasticities. The empirical consensus for prime-age, primary earners places compensated elasticities in the 0.1–0.4 range, with many microsimulation models (e.g., CBO-style) using values around 0.25. Decile 6 earners are solidly middle-class, facing moderate effective marginal tax rates, and are not at the intensive margin extremes seen in very low or very high deciles. I center my belief at 0.25 with meaningful uncertainty spanning roughly 0.05–0.50 across the 90% interval.
